stem: Which statement best reflects the lifeblood doctrine?
choice_a: Taxes may be offset against the government's unpaid obligations to the taxpayer
choice_b: Courts may freely enjoin the collection of taxes upon a showing of inconvenience
choice_c: The prompt and certain availability of taxes is essential because the government cannot continue to exist without revenue
choice_d: The government is bound by the mistakes of its revenue officers under the principle of estoppel
explanation: Under the lifeblood doctrine, taxes are the lifeblood of the State and their prompt and certain availability is an imperious need. This is why collection generally cannot be enjoined, taxes are not subject to set-off, and the State is not estopped by errors of its agents.

stem: Which of the three inherent powers of the State may also be exercised by public service companies and public utilities?
explanation: Eminent domain may be delegated to and exercised by public service companies and public utilities. Taxation and police power are exercised only by the government or its political subdivisions.

stem: The BASIS of taxation, as distinguished from its theory, is:
choice_a: The reciprocal duties of protection by the State and support by its citizens
choice_b: The principle that the existence of government is a necessity
choice_c: The rule that taxes are the lifeblood of the nation
choice_d: The doctrine that the power to tax involves the power to destroy
explanation: The basis of taxation is the benefits-protection theory: the citizen supports the State and the State protects the citizen. The theory of taxation, by contrast, is necessity or the lifeblood concept.

stem: Under the canons of a sound tax system, fiscal adequacy means:
choice_a: Tax laws must be capable of convenient, just and effective administration
choice_b: The tax burden must be proportionate to the taxpayer's ability to pay
choice_c: Tax rates must be identical in every province and city
choice_d: Sources of revenue must be sufficient and elastic enough to meet the expanding expenditures of government
explanation: Fiscal adequacy requires that revenue sources, as a whole, be adequate and flexible enough to fund government needs. Choice A describes administrative feasibility and choice B describes theoretical justice.

stem: Congress enacts a tax measure that proves extremely costly and burdensome for both the BIR and taxpayers to administer. Which is correct?
choice_a: The law is void for violating the principle of administrative feasibility
choice_b: The law remains valid because administrative feasibility is a canon of a sound tax system, not a constitutional requirement
choice_c: The law is void unless the Department of Finance certifies it as collectible
choice_d: The law is automatically suspended until simplified regulations are issued
explanation: Non-observance of administrative feasibility, one of the canons of a sound tax system, does not by itself invalidate a tax law. Only violations of inherent or constitutional limitations render a tax law void.

stem: Theoretical justice as a principle of a sound tax system requires that:
choice_a: The tax burden be proportionate to the taxpayer's ability to pay
choice_b: Revenue sources be elastic enough to fund all government programs
choice_c: Tax laws be convenient and economical to administer
choice_d: Taxes be collected exclusively from corporations with large profits
explanation: Theoretical justice means taxes must be based on the taxpayer's ability to pay, echoing the constitutional directive to evolve a progressive system of taxation.

stem: Which of the following is an INHERENT limitation on the power of taxation?
choice_a: No person shall be deprived of property without due process of law
choice_b: No person shall be denied the equal protection of the laws
choice_c: No law impairing the obligation of contracts shall be passed
choice_d: Taxation may be exercised only within the territorial jurisdiction of the taxing authority
explanation: Territoriality is an inherent limitation that exists even without the Constitution. Due process, equal protection and non-impairment are constitutional limitations.

stem: Which delegation of the taxing power is INVALID?
choice_a: Congress authorizes the President, within specified limits, to adjust tariff rates under the flexible tariff clause
choice_b: The Constitution grants local government units the power to create their own sources of revenue
choice_c: A statute authorizes the Secretary of Finance to select which businesses to tax and to fix the applicable tax rates
choice_d: The BIR issues regulations prescribing the manner of filing returns under an existing tax law
explanation: The power to select the subjects of taxation and fix rates is purely legislative and cannot be delegated to an executive official. Tariff flexibility, local taxation and purely administrative implementation are recognized exceptions to non-delegation.

stem: The constitutional rule that taxation shall be uniform means:
choice_a: All taxpayers must pay exactly the same amount of tax
choice_b: All subjects or objects belonging to the same class are taxed at the same rate
choice_c: A single tax rate must apply nationwide to all kinds of income
choice_d: Taxes must always be progressive in structure
explanation: Uniformity requires that persons or things belonging to the same class be taxed at the same rate; valid classification is allowed. It does not require equal amounts from every taxpayer.

stem: A religious order owns a building whose ground floor is leased to a commercial bakery while the upper floors are used as a convent. For real property tax purposes:
choice_a: Only the portion actually, directly and exclusively used for religious purposes is exempt; the leased ground floor is taxable
choice_b: The entire building is exempt because the owner is a religious order
choice_c: The entire building is taxable because part of it is used commercially
choice_d: The building is exempt only if the rental income is donated to charity
explanation: The constitutional exemption attaches to actual, direct and exclusive USE of the property for religious, charitable or educational purposes, not to ownership. The portion leased out for commerce loses the exemption.

stem: A city imposes a local business tax on a store whose income is already subject to national income tax. The two impositions constitute:
choice_a: Direct double taxation, which is prohibited by the Constitution
choice_b: Direct double taxation, which is nevertheless valid
choice_c: No double taxation in any sense of the term
choice_d: Indirect (broad sense) double taxation, which is not legally prohibited
explanation: Because the taxes are imposed by different taxing authorities (national government and city) and are different in kind, this is double taxation only in the broad sense, which is not prohibited. Direct double taxation requires the same subject, purpose, authority, jurisdiction, period and kind of tax.

stem: Which of the following is NOT a method of mitigating or eliminating international double taxation?
choice_a: Entering into tax treaties with other states
choice_b: Shifting the tax burden to consumers through higher prices
choice_c: Allowing a tax credit for foreign taxes paid
choice_d: Exempting income earned abroad from domestic tax
explanation: Tax treaties, foreign tax credits and exemption of foreign income are recognized relief measures against international juridical double taxation. Shifting merely transfers the economic burden and does not relieve double taxation.

stem: A city collects P500 from every bicycle-for-hire operator to fund a registry and periodic safety inspections, the amount approximating the cost of regulation. The exaction is:
choice_a: A tax, because it is collected by a local government
choice_b: A toll, because it relates to the use of streets
choice_c: A license fee imposed under the police power
choice_d: A special assessment on personal property
explanation: An exaction imposed primarily for regulation, in an amount commensurate to the cost of regulation and supervision, is a license fee under the police power rather than a tax for revenue.

stem: Which statement properly characterizes a special assessment?
choice_a: It is levied only on lands within the area benefited by a public improvement and is based on the special benefit received
choice_b: It is a personal liability of the landowner even if the land receives no benefit
choice_c: It is imposed on persons, property and business to raise general revenue
choice_d: It is a periodic charge for the privilege of doing business
explanation: A special assessment is an enforced proportional contribution levied only on land, based wholly on the special benefit the land derives from a public improvement; it is not a personal liability and is not for general revenue.

stem: A corporation with a final and executory deficiency income tax assessment seeks to offset the assessment against the national government's unpaid bill for goods the corporation delivered. May legal compensation take place?
choice_a: Yes, because both obligations are money claims that are due and demandable
choice_b: Yes, provided the assessment has become final
choice_c: Yes, but only with the approval of the Commission on Audit
choice_d: No, because taxes are not contractual obligations and the government and the taxpayer are not mutual creditors and debtors of each other
explanation: Taxes arise from law, not contract, so the rules on legal compensation do not apply. The lifeblood doctrine bars set-off of taxes against ordinary claims against the government.

stem: Which act constitutes tax EVASION rather than tax avoidance?
choice_a: Electing the 8% income tax option to legally lower the total tax due
choice_b: Recording fictitious purchase invoices and deliberately underdeclaring sales
choice_c: Deferring the perfection of a sale to the following taxable year
choice_d: Claiming the optional standard deduction in lieu of itemized deductions
explanation: Evasion involves the use of illegal means, such as fictitious deductions and deliberate underdeclaration, coupled with intent to evade. The other choices are legal tax minimization (avoidance).

stem: Faced with a higher excise tax, a manufacturer upgrades its machinery and recovers the tax through lower production costs without raising its selling price. This form of escape from taxation is:
explanation: Transformation occurs when the producer absorbs the tax and recovers it through more efficient production rather than passing it on. Shifting transfers the burden to buyers, while capitalization reduces the price of property purchased.

stem: A nonresident citizen granted a loan to a nonresident foreign corporation. The loan papers were signed in Manila and the interest was remitted to the lender's Manila bank account. For Philippine income tax purposes, the interest is:
choice_a: Not taxable, because the situs of interest income is the residence of the debtor, which is abroad, and a nonresident citizen is taxed only on Philippine-source income
choice_b: Taxable, because the creditor is a Filipino citizen
choice_c: Subject to the 20% final withholding tax on interest
choice_d: Taxable, because the contract was executed in Manila
explanation: Interest income is sourced at the residence of the DEBTOR. Since the debtor is a nonresident foreign corporation, the interest is foreign-source, and a nonresident citizen is taxed only on income from Philippine sources.

stem: A nonresident alien was paid a fee by a domestic corporation for consulting services performed entirely in Singapore. The fee is:
choice_a: Philippine-source income because the payor is a domestic corporation
choice_b: Subject to the 25% final withholding tax on gross income
choice_c: Subject to the graduated income tax rates
choice_d: Foreign-source income not taxable to the nonresident alien, because compensation for services is sourced at the place of performance
explanation: The situs of income from services is the place where the services are performed, regardless of the payor's residence or where payment is made. Services rendered abroad by a nonresident alien produce foreign-source income beyond Philippine taxing jurisdiction.

stem: On the retroactive application of tax laws, the general rule is that tax laws:
choice_a: Are always applied retroactively because taxes are the lifeblood of the State
choice_b: Operate prospectively, unless the legislature clearly intends retroactivity and the retroactive application is not harsh or oppressive
choice_c: May never be applied retroactively under any circumstance
choice_d: Apply retroactively by default because they are civil in nature
explanation: Tax laws are applied prospectively as a rule. Retroactive application is permitted only when clearly provided by the legislature and provided it does not amount to a harsh, oppressive deprivation of due process.

stem: Philippine internal revenue laws are properly characterized as:
choice_a: Penal in nature, because violations are punishable by imprisonment
choice_b: Political in nature, because they are enacted by Congress
choice_c: Civil in nature, which is why they remained effective even during enemy occupation
choice_d: Criminal in nature, because the State prosecutes violators
explanation: Internal revenue laws are civil, not penal or political, in nature. Hence they continued in force during the enemy occupation of the Philippines and the rule against ex post facto laws does not generally apply to them.

stem: A resident citizen earned compensation income in the Philippines and rental income from a condominium unit located in Singapore. Which income is subject to Philippine income tax?
choice_c: Both the compensation and the rental income
choice_d: Neither, because foreign income is always exempt
explanation: A resident citizen is taxable on income from all sources within and without the Philippines. Hence both the local compensation and the foreign rental income are taxable in the Philippines.

stem: A non-resident citizen working as a seafarer on an ocean-going vessel earned salaries abroad and interest from a Philippine bank deposit. For Philippine income tax, the non-resident citizen is taxable on income from sources:
explanation: A non-resident citizen (including a qualified seafarer) is taxable only on income derived from sources within the Philippines. The foreign salary is not taxed here, while the Philippine bank interest is.

stem: A non-resident alien not engaged in trade or business in the Philippines received P500,000 of gross income from Philippine sources during the year. The income tax due is:
explanation: A non-resident alien not engaged in trade or business is taxed at a flat final rate of 25% on gross income from Philippine sources. P500,000 x 25% = P125,000, with no deductions or personal exemptions allowed.

stem: Which of the following individuals is taxed using the graduated income tax table on net taxable income?
choice_a: A non-resident alien not engaged in trade or business in the Philippines
choice_c: A non-resident citizen on income earned abroad
choice_d: A non-resident alien engaged in trade or business in the Philippines
explanation: A non-resident alien engaged in trade or business in the Philippines is taxed on net taxable Philippine income using the graduated table. A non-resident alien not engaged in trade or business is taxed at 25% final on gross.

stem: An employee received during the year a 13th-month pay of P60,000 and other benefits totaling P45,000. Applying the P90,000 exclusion cap, how much of these benefits is included in taxable compensation income?
explanation: The 13th-month pay and other benefits are excluded only up to P90,000. Total benefits = P60,000 + P45,000 = P105,000; the excess of P105,000 - P90,000 = P15,000 is included in taxable compensation.

stem: Which of the following amounts received by a rank-and-file employee is EXCLUDED from gross compensation income?
choice_a: Overtime pay of a regular employee
choice_c: A productivity incentive bonus of P20,000 with no other benefits
choice_d: Mandatory SSS, PhilHealth, and Pag-IBIG contributions withheld from salary
explanation: The employee share in mandatory SSS, PhilHealth, and Pag-IBIG contributions is excluded from gross compensation income. Overtime, commissions, and bonuses are generally taxable, subject only to the P90,000 benefits cap.

stem: A statutory minimum wage earner received basic minimum wage plus overtime pay, holiday pay, night-shift differential, and hazard pay during the year. The income tax treatment is:
choice_a: The basic wage is exempt but the OT, holiday, night, and hazard pay are taxable
choice_b: All of the items are taxable at graduated rates
choice_c: Only the hazard pay is exempt
choice_d: All of the items are exempt from income tax
explanation: A statutory minimum wage earner is exempt from income tax on the statutory minimum wage, and the exemption likewise covers holiday pay, overtime pay, night-shift differential pay, and hazard pay.

stem: Using the graduated table (0% up to P250,000; 15% over P250,000 to P400,000; 20% over P400,000 to P800,000; 25% over P800,000 to P2,000,000), a resident citizen has net taxable income of P650,000. The income tax due is:

Using the graduated table (0% up to P250,000; 15% over P250,000 to P400,000; 20% over P400,000 to P800,000; 25% over P800,000 to P2,000,000; 30% over P2,000,000 to P8,000,000), a taxpayer has net taxable income of P1,200,000. The income tax due is:
Using the graduated table (cumulative tax on P8,000,000 is P2,202,500, then 35% on the excess over P8,000,000), a taxpayer has net taxable income of P10,000,000. The income tax due is:
A self-employed professional with gross receipts of P2,400,000 (not VAT-registered, below the VAT threshold) elects the 8% income tax option. The income tax due under the 8% option is:
explanation: The 8% option applies to gross sales or receipts in excess of P250,000 in lieu of graduated tax and the 3% percentage tax. (P2,400,000 - P250,000) x 8% = P2,150,000 x 8% = P172,000.

stem: A purely self-employed individual (not VAT-registered, below the VAT threshold) has gross receipts of P1,000,000 and deductible expenses of P300,000. Under the graduated table, tax on the first P250,000 net is P0, 15% over P250,000 to P400,000, and 20% over P400,000 to P800,000. Comparing the graduated tax with the 8% option, the lower tax is:
explanation: Net taxable income = P700,000; graduated tax = P22,500 + 20% of P300,000 = P82,500. The 8% option = (P1,000,000 - P250,000) x 8% = P60,000, which is lower.

stem: A mixed-income earner has taxable compensation income of P500,000 and gross receipts from business of P800,000. He elects the 8% option on business income. In computing the 8% business tax, the P250,000 reduction:
choice_a: Is deducted from the gross receipts because every taxpayer gets it
choice_b: Is NOT deducted because it is already incorporated in the graduated tax on compensation
choice_c: Is deducted only from the compensation income
choice_d: Is split equally between compensation and business
explanation: For a mixed-income earner electing 8% on business, the 8% is applied to the full gross sales or receipts WITHOUT the P250,000 reduction, since that amount is already built into the graduated rates applied to the compensation income.

stem: A mixed-income earner elects 8% on business. His gross business receipts are P800,000 and he has no other business deductions. The 8% income tax on the business income is:
explanation: For a mixed-income earner, the 8% is applied to gross receipts WITHOUT the P250,000 reduction. P800,000 x 8% = P64,000.

stem: Which condition must be satisfied before an individual may elect the 8% income tax option on gross sales or receipts?
choice_b: Gross sales or receipts must exceed the P3,000,000 VAT threshold
choice_c: The taxpayer must not be VAT-registered and must be below the P3,000,000 VAT threshold
choice_d: The taxpayer must be a non-resident alien not engaged in trade or business
explanation: The 8% option is available only to non-VAT taxpayers whose gross sales or receipts do not exceed the P3,000,000 VAT threshold and who are not otherwise required to register for VAT or subject to other percentage taxes.

stem: A resident citizen received P100,000 interest income from a Philippine peso bank deposit. The final tax to be withheld is:
explanation: Interest income from a Philippine peso bank deposit of a resident citizen is subject to a 20% final withholding tax. P100,000 x 20% = P20,000.

stem: A resident citizen earned P200,000 of royalties from a literary work (a published book). The final tax on this royalty is:
explanation: Royalties on books, literary works, and musical compositions are subject to a reduced 10% final tax. P200,000 x 10% = P20,000, rather than the 20% rate that applies to other royalties.

stem: A resident citizen received P150,000 of cash dividends from a domestic corporation. The final tax is:
explanation: Cash dividends received by a citizen or resident from a domestic corporation are subject to a 10% final tax. P150,000 x 10% = P15,000.

stem: A resident citizen earned interest of P80,000 from a depository bank under the foreign currency deposit system (FCDU). The final tax is:
explanation: Interest income of a resident from a foreign currency deposit (FCDU/EFCDU) is subject to a 15% final tax. P80,000 x 15% = P12,000.

stem: Which of the following passive income items earned by a resident citizen is NOT subject to a final withholding tax but is instead reported in the annual income tax return at graduated rates?
choice_a: Interest on a Philippine peso bank deposit
choice_b: Royalties on a patent licensed to a local manufacturer
choice_c: Net rental income from a commercial building
choice_d: Cash dividends from a domestic corporation
explanation: Rental income is active or ordinary income reported in the annual return at graduated rates. Bank-deposit interest, royalties, and domestic dividends are subject to final withholding taxes.

stem: An individual sold a residential lot held as a capital asset for P5,000,000. The fair market value per the zonal valuation is P5,800,000 and the assessed value is P4,500,000. The capital gains tax is:
explanation: The 6% capital gains tax on real property classified as a capital asset is based on the higher of the gross selling price and the fair market value (zonal or assessed). The highest is the zonal value of P5,800,000 x 6% = P348,000.

stem: An individual sold shares of stock of a domestic corporation (not traded through the local stock exchange) for P900,000. The shares were acquired for P500,000. The capital gains tax is:
explanation: Sale of unlisted shares of a domestic corporation is subject to a 15% capital gains tax on the net capital gain. Net gain = P900,000 - P500,000 = P400,000; CGT = P400,000 x 15% = P60,000.

stem: An individual taxpayer held a capital asset (not real property or shares) for 18 months before selling it at a gain of P200,000. In computing taxable capital gains for an individual, the amount of gain to be reported is:
explanation: For an individual, a capital asset held for more than 12 months is a long-term capital asset and only 50% of the gain or loss is taken into account. P200,000 x 50% = P100,000.

stem: An individual taxpayer incurred a net capital loss of P50,000 in the prior year, when net income was positive. The net capital loss carryover may be deducted:
choice_b: Only in the same year it was incurred
choice_c: For the next three succeeding years
choice_d: In the next succeeding taxable year only, not exceeding the prior-year net income
explanation: An individual may carry a net capital loss over to the next succeeding taxable year only (one year), treated as a short-term loss, and the carryover cannot exceed the net income in the year the loss was sustained.

stem: Substituted filing of the annual income tax return is available to an employee who:
choice_a: Has two or more employers during the year
choice_b: Earns purely compensation income from one employer for the year with correct tax withheld
choice_d: Earns mixed compensation and business income
explanation: Substituted filing applies to a qualified employee receiving purely compensation income from only one employer for the calendar year, whose tax has been correctly withheld; the employer's annual information return serves as the employee's return.

stem: A managerial employee received the free use of a company residential house, where the employer leases the unit at a quarterly rental of P130,000. The monetary value of the benefit is 50% of the rental. The fringe benefit tax for the quarter (35% on the grossed-up monetary value) is:
explanation: Monetary value = P130,000 x 50% = P65,000. Grossed-up monetary value = P65,000 / 65% = P100,000; FBT = P100,000 x 35% = P35,000.

stem: The fringe benefit tax is imposed on fringe benefits granted to:
explanation: The fringe benefit tax applies only to fringe benefits granted to managerial or supervisory employees. Benefits given to rank-and-file employees are part of their compensation income subject to regular income tax.

stem: A self-employed individual with gross receipts of P1,500,000 elects the Optional Standard Deduction. The OSD for this individual is:
explanation: For an individual, the OSD is 40% of gross sales or gross receipts, not of gross income. P1,500,000 x 40% = P600,000.

stem: A resident alien employed in the Philippines also earns rental income from property located in his home country abroad. The resident alien is taxable on income from:
choice_a: Sources within and without the Philippines
explanation: A resident alien, like a non-resident citizen, is taxable only on income from sources within the Philippines. The foreign rental income is therefore not subject to Philippine income tax.

stem: A domestic corporation is taxable on income derived from:
choice_c: Sources within and without the Philippines
explanation: A domestic corporation is taxable on its income from all sources, both within and without the Philippines. Resident and non-resident foreign corporations are taxed only on Philippine-source income.

stem: A resident foreign corporation operating a branch in the Philippines is taxable on:
choice_b: Gross income at a flat 25% final tax
choice_c: Income earned at its head office abroad
explanation: A resident foreign corporation (such as a Philippine branch of a foreign company) is taxed only on income derived from sources within the Philippines, generally at the regular corporate income tax rate.

stem: A non-resident foreign corporation received P2,000,000 of gross income from Philippine sources. The income tax due is:
explanation: A non-resident foreign corporation is subject to a 25% final tax on gross income from Philippine sources. P2,000,000 x 25% = P500,000, with no deductions allowed.

stem: A domestic corporation has net taxable income of P4,000,000 and total assets of P80,000,000 (excluding the land on which its office stands). Applying the two-condition test, the regular corporate income tax due is:
explanation: The 20% rate applies only if net taxable income is at most P5,000,000 AND total assets (excluding land) are at most P100,000,000. Both conditions are met, so RCIT = P4,000,000 x 20% = P800,000.

stem: A domestic corporation has net taxable income of P4,500,000 but total assets of P120,000,000 (excluding land). The regular corporate income tax due is:
explanation: The 20% rate requires BOTH net taxable income at most P5,000,000 AND total assets (excluding land) at most P100,000,000. Because total assets exceed P100,000,000, the 25% rate applies: P4,500,000 x 25% = P1,125,000.

stem: A domestic corporation has net taxable income of P6,000,000 and total assets of P50,000,000 (excluding land). The regular corporate income tax due is:
explanation: Although total assets are below P100,000,000, the net taxable income of P6,000,000 exceeds the P5,000,000 ceiling. Because BOTH conditions are not met, the 25% rate applies: P6,000,000 x 25% = P1,500,000.

stem: A domestic corporation in its fifth year of operations has gross income of P10,000,000 and net taxable income of P400,000. RCIT is 25%. The income tax payable is:
explanation: MCIT of 2% of gross income applies from the 4th taxable year and the corporation pays the higher of RCIT and MCIT. RCIT = P400,000 x 25% = P100,000; MCIT = P10,000,000 x 2% = P200,000. The higher, P200,000, is payable.

stem: A domestic corporation in its sixth year has gross income of P8,000,000 and net taxable income of P2,000,000 subject to 25% RCIT. The income tax payable is:
explanation: RCIT = P2,000,000 x 25% = P500,000; MCIT = P8,000,000 x 2% = P160,000. The corporation pays the higher amount, which is the RCIT of P500,000.

stem: The minimum corporate income tax (MCIT) is imposed beginning the:
choice_c: Fourth taxable year immediately following the year of commencement of operations
choice_d: Year the corporation first reports a net loss
explanation: The 2% MCIT is imposed beginning on the fourth taxable year immediately following the year in which the corporation commenced its business operations.

stem: Any excess of the MCIT over the RCIT may be carried forward and credited against the RCIT for the:
choice_d: Next three immediately succeeding taxable years
explanation: The excess of MCIT over the normal RCIT in any year may be carried forward and credited against the normal income tax due for the three immediately succeeding taxable years.

stem: In Year 1, a corporation paid MCIT of P200,000 while its RCIT was P50,000, creating an excess MCIT of P150,000. In Year 2, RCIT is P300,000 and MCIT is P120,000. After applying the excess MCIT carryover, the income tax still payable in Year 2 is:
explanation: In Year 2, RCIT P300,000 exceeds MCIT P120,000, so RCIT applies. The prior excess MCIT of P150,000 is credited: P300,000 - P150,000 = P150,000 still payable.

stem: Dividends received by a domestic corporation from another domestic corporation are:
explanation: Inter-corporate dividends received by a domestic corporation from another domestic corporation are exempt from income tax to avoid taxing the same earnings multiple times.

stem: A net operating loss carryover (NOLCO) for a corporation may generally be carried over as a deduction from gross income for the:
choice_b: Three consecutive taxable years immediately following the year of loss
explanation: A net operating loss not previously offset may be carried over as a deduction for the next three consecutive taxable years immediately following the year the loss was incurred.

stem: Which statement about NOLCO is correct?
choice_a: NOLCO may be deducted in a year the corporation elects the OSD
choice_b: NOLCO may be deducted against the MCIT base
choice_c: NOLCO is allowed only when the taxpayer uses itemized deductions and is not applied against the MCIT
choice_d: NOLCO may be carried over indefinitely
explanation: NOLCO is allowed only when itemized deductions are claimed; it cannot be deducted in a year the OSD is used, and it cannot be applied against the gross-income base used for the MCIT.

stem: A Philippine branch of a foreign corporation remitted P3,000,000 of its after-tax profits to its head office abroad. The branch profit remittance tax is:
explanation: Profits remitted by a branch to its foreign head office are subject to a 15% branch profit remittance tax on the amount applied or earmarked for remittance. P3,000,000 x 15% = P450,000.

stem: A proprietary educational institution has net income of P8,000,000, of which P2,000,000 (25% of total) comes from unrelated trade or business. Because unrelated income does not exceed 50% of total income, the income tax due is:
explanation: A proprietary educational institution enjoys the preferential 10% rate provided unrelated income does not exceed 50% of total income. Here unrelated income is 25% of total, so 10% applies to the entire income: P8,000,000 x 10% = P800,000.

stem: A nonprofit hospital earned total income of P10,000,000, of which P6,000,000 (60% of total) came from unrelated trade or business. Applying the predominance test, the income tax due is:
explanation: Under the predominance test, if unrelated income exceeds 50% of total income, the entire taxable income is subject to the regular 25% rate rather than 10%. P10,000,000 x 25% = P2,500,000.

stem: A corporation organized and operated exclusively for religious purposes under Section 30 earned interest income of P500,000 from placing its idle funds in a time deposit. This interest income is:
choice_a: Exempt because the corporation is a Section 30 entity
choice_c: Subject to tax because income of whatever kind from any of its properties or activities for profit is taxable
explanation: Although Section 30 organizations are generally exempt on income related to their exempt purpose, the last paragraph of Section 30 makes income of whatever kind from any of their properties or from any activity conducted for profit taxable, regardless of disposition.

stem: A domestic corporation with gross income of P5,000,000 (gross sales P12,000,000 less cost of sales P7,000,000) elects the OSD. The optional standard deduction is:
explanation: For a corporation, the OSD is 40% of gross income (gross sales less cost of sales), not of gross sales. P5,000,000 x 40% = P2,000,000.

stem: The improperly accumulated earnings tax (IAET) under the prior law is, as of 2026:
choice_a: Imposed at 10% on improperly accumulated taxable income
choice_b: Imposed at 15% on improperly accumulated taxable income
explanation: The CREATE Act (RA 11534) repealed the improperly accumulated earnings tax, so it is no longer imposed.

stem: A domestic corporation in its third year of operations is:
choice_c: Not yet subject to MCIT because the fourth year has not been reached
explanation: The MCIT begins only in the fourth taxable year following commencement of operations. In its third year, the corporation is subject to RCIT but not yet to MCIT.

stem: A domestic corporation has gross income of P20,000,000, allowable itemized deductions of P19,200,000, and is in its seventh year. RCIT is 25%. The income tax payable is:
explanation: Net taxable income = P20,000,000 - P19,200,000 = P800,000; RCIT = P800,000 x 25% = P200,000. MCIT = P20,000,000 x 2% = P400,000. The higher amount, MCIT P400,000, is payable.

stem: Which of the following is a resident foreign corporation?
choice_a: A corporation organized under Philippine laws
choice_b: A foreign corporation with no Philippine business but receiving Philippine dividends
choice_c: A foreign airline maintaining a Philippine branch and engaged in business here
choice_d: A foreign corporation that merely owns shares in a domestic corporation
explanation: A resident foreign corporation is a foreign corporation engaged in trade or business within the Philippines, such as a foreign airline operating a Philippine branch. One not engaged in Philippine business is a non-resident foreign corporation.

stem: A domestic corporation in its eighth year has a net loss of P1,000,000 but gross income of P9,000,000. RCIT is 25%. The income tax payable is:
explanation: With a net loss, RCIT = P0, but the MCIT still applies once past the fourth year. MCIT = P9,000,000 x 2% = P180,000, which is the tax payable. The corporation also generates a NOLCO for future years.

stem: The total assets used in the small-corporation two-condition test (for the 20% rate) are measured:
choice_a: Including the value of the land on which the office is situated
choice_c: Excluding the value of the land on which the office, plant, or equipment is situated
choice_d: Based on gross sales for the year
explanation: The P100,000,000 total-asset threshold for the 20% rate excludes the value of the land on which the corporation's office, plant, and equipment are situated.

stem: A domestic corporation reported gross income of P15,000,000 and net taxable income of P5,000,000 with total assets of P90,000,000 (excluding land), in its tenth year. The income tax payable is:
explanation: Net taxable income is exactly P5,000,000 (at most P5,000,000) and total assets P90,000,000 are at most P100,000,000, so the 20% rate applies: RCIT = P5,000,000 x 20% = P1,000,000. MCIT = P15,000,000 x 2% = P300,000; the higher is RCIT P1,000,000.

stem: Dividends received by a resident foreign corporation from a domestic corporation are generally:
explanation: Dividends received by a resident foreign corporation from a domestic corporation are generally exempt from income tax, similar to the treatment of inter-corporate dividends between domestic corporations.

stem: A domestic corporation has gross income of P3,000,000, itemized deductions of P2,600,000, and is in its second year of operations. RCIT is 25%. The income tax payable is:
explanation: In the second year, MCIT does not yet apply, so only RCIT is computed. Net taxable income = P3,000,000 - P2,600,000 = P400,000; RCIT = P400,000 x 25% = P100,000.

stem: Which of the following items is NOT deducted in determining the gross income base of the 2% MCIT for a trading corporation?
explanation: The MCIT is based on gross income (net sales less cost of sales). Selling and administrative operating expenses are NOT deducted in arriving at the gross income base, unlike in the RCIT computation.

stem: A general professional partnership (GPP) is:
choice_a: Taxed as a corporation at 25% RCIT
choice_b: Exempt as a separate entity, with partners taxed on their distributive shares
choice_c: Subject to a 10% final tax on its net income
choice_d: Subject to the 8% income tax option as an entity
explanation: A general professional partnership is not subject to income tax as an entity. Instead, each partner is taxed in his individual capacity on his distributive share, whether or not actually distributed.

stem: An ordinary (commercial) business partnership is, for income tax purposes:
choice_b: Subject to the graduated individual table
choice_c: Taxed as a corporation, with partners' shares treated as dividends
choice_d: Subject only to the 3% percentage tax
explanation: An ordinary or commercial partnership is taxed like a corporation. The partners' distributive shares are treated as dividends and are subject to the 10% final tax.

stem: A commercial partnership had distributable net income after tax of P1,000,000, shared equally by two individual resident partners. The final tax on each partner's share is:
explanation: Each partner's share is P500,000, treated as a dividend from an entity taxed as a corporation, subject to a 10% final tax. P500,000 x 10% = P50,000 per partner.

stem: A GPP has net professional income of P2,000,000 shared equally between two partners. The GPP claimed itemized deductions at the partnership level. In computing his own taxable income, each partner:
choice_a: Reports a P1,000,000 distributive share and cannot again deduct the same partnership expenses
choice_b: May again deduct the partnership-level expenses against his share
choice_c: Pays a 10% final tax on the P1,000,000 share
choice_d: Is exempt because the GPP already paid the tax
explanation: The GPP determines net income at the partnership level (itemized or OSD). Each partner reports his distributive share as part of gross income and cannot again deduct the expenses already claimed by the GPP.

stem: A joint venture formed for undertaking a construction project, where the co-venturers are licensed local contractors, is:
choice_c: Not taxable as a corporation; the co-venturers are taxed separately on their shares
choice_d: Exempt from all taxes including the percentage tax
explanation: A joint venture for construction projects (and one for energy operations under a service contract with the government) is NOT treated as a taxable corporation. The co-venturers report their respective shares in their own returns.

stem: A joint venture formed by two corporations to operate a chain of retail stores (not construction or energy operations) is:
choice_c: Subject only to the percentage tax
choice_d: Taxed under the graduated individual table
explanation: A joint venture that is not for construction or for energy or petroleum operations under a government service contract is treated as a taxable corporation subject to corporate income tax.

stem: Two heirs inherited a parcel of land and merely held it as co-owners while collecting rent, dividing the income between them without reinvesting. The co-ownership is:
choice_a: Generally not taxable as a separate entity; each co-owner reports his share
choice_d: Subject to the 8% option as an entity
explanation: A co-ownership for the mere preservation of inherited property and collection of its income is generally not a taxable entity. Each co-owner reports his respective share of the income in his own return.

stem: Two heirs who inherited property continued to invest the rental income jointly in additional income-producing ventures for profit. The arrangement may be treated as:
choice_b: An unregistered partnership taxable as a corporation
explanation: When co-owners do not divide the income but instead reinvest it in additional income-producing ventures for profit, the co-ownership is converted into an unregistered partnership taxable as a corporation.

stem: An estate under judicial settlement is, for income tax purposes:
choice_a: A separate taxpayer using the graduated income tax table
choice_c: Taxed as a corporation at 25% RCIT
explanation: An estate under judicial settlement is a separate taxpayer and computes its income tax using the graduated table applicable to individuals during the period of settlement.

stem: An estate under judicial settlement earned net income of P800,000 and distributed P300,000 to an heir during the year. In computing the taxable income of the estate, the P300,000 distributed to the heir is:
choice_a: Deductible by the estate and taxable to the heir
choice_d: Exempt to both estate and heir
explanation: Income of the estate that is properly paid or credited to an heir during the year is deductible by the estate and is taxable to the heir who receives it, avoiding double taxation of the same income.

stem: An irrevocable trust is treated as:
choice_a: Income always taxable to the grantor
choice_b: A separate taxpayer computing tax using the graduated table
explanation: An irrevocable trust is a separate taxpayer and computes its income tax using the graduated individual table, similar to an estate under judicial settlement.

stem: Income of a revocable trust (where the grantor reserved the power to revest title in himself) is taxable to:
choice_b: The trust as a separate entity
choice_d: No one, because it is exempt
explanation: Where the title to any part of the trust corpus may be revested in the grantor (a revocable trust), the income of that part is taxed to the grantor and not to the trust.

stem: A grantor created two trusts in favor of the same beneficiary. For income tax purposes, the income of the two trusts is:
choice_a: Consolidated and taxed as if it were the income of a single trust
choice_b: Taxed separately, each enjoying its own exemption
choice_d: Exempt because there are multiple trusts
explanation: Where two or more trusts are created by the same grantor for the same beneficiary, the taxable income of all the trusts is consolidated and the tax computed as on a single trust, with any excess tax apportioned among the trustees.
